notes: Emergent social construct; Bandura's four efficacy channels require physical collaboration and shared professional identity.
AI dashboards can scaffold prediction and tracking; student must do the self-assessment act or mechanism hollows out.
AI platforms can continuously monitor and flag tier transitions; targeted instruction must be physically delivered by trained humans.
Physical peer-to-peer teaching exchange is the causal mechanism; AI optimizing group composition touches only logistics.

influence: Strategy to integrate with prior knowledge
notes: AI pre-diagnostic tasks can map active schema; customized analogies require accuracy about what student actually knows.
AI adaptive micro-scaffolds can foster immediate confidence; durable self-efficacy is built through human encouragement over time.
Student perception of competence, trustworthiness, and goodwill is strictly interpersonal; AI fluency is not teacher credibility.
AI computer vision can map talk-time, questioning patterns, and engagement; human teacher must interpret and adjust practice.
AI generates near- and far-transfer problem sets; explicit modeling of cross-domain principles requires human instruction.
Socio-cognitive friction and shared community context require physical peer presence; AI can map participation patterns and flag gaps.
AI tutoring platforms can adapt hint specificity and difficulty in real-time; systematic fading required to prevent permanent dependency.
Ericsson-model deliberate practice requires expert diagnosis of specific errors; AI handles adaptive difficulty but not expert qualitative feedback.
Critical mechanism conflict: if AI summarizes for the student, student does not build the capacity. AI must evaluate student summaries, not produce them.
AI can structure prediction prompts; mechanism conflict if AI does the planning for the student rather than requiring student to plan.
Mechanism conflict risk: AI must force student verbal elaboration, not produce elaboration on student's behalf.
AI prompt systems generate self-evaluation questions; deep metacognitive internalization of error requires teacher-guided think-alouds.
AI avatars can model predict/clarify/question/summarize; the peer-teaching social exchange requires physical human presence.

Most-cited EdTech claim. Mechanism conflict: AI feedback can enable system-gaming (clicking hints for answers). One-third of feedback studies show negative effects. Vendor claims routinely strip this caveat.
AI onboarding engines can prompt explicit goal formulation; mechanism conflict if AI sets goals for student rather than requiring student to formulate them.
Rich problem-solving scenarios can be AI-generated; group facilitation and modeling of problem-solving heuristics requires human teacher.
Mechanism conflict: AI must evaluate student-produced outlines, not generate outlines for students.
AI can visualize conceptual hierarchies from student text; student must do the conceptual construction or mechanism is bypassed.
AI provides diverse divergent-thinking prompts; subjective elaboration and collaborative creative evaluation require human facilitation.
Behavior modification requires physical presence, relational authority, and embodied social judgment; AI monitoring is peripheral.
AI can co-construct rubrics from historical baselines; student self-appraisal accuracy requires human calibration and validation.
Critical mechanism conflict: perpetual AI Socratic prompting creates cognitive dependency. Without systematic fading, AI acts as external regulator, hollowing out independent metacognitive capacity.
AI can calibrate task difficulty to zone of proximal development; mechanism conflict if AI sets goals rather than supporting student to set them.
Embodied physical stimulation is the causal mechanism; AI has no role in the core intervention.
Mechanism conflict risk: AI must scaffold student self-monitoring, not monitor for the student.
Embodied civic action and community relationship-building cannot be replicated by AI; mechanism is entirely human and contextual.
Software-defined mastery often assesses procedural retrieval only. Deep conceptual transfer and self-pacing accountability require human oversight. Vendor claims of 100% mastery are unsupported.
Mechanism conflict: AI must require student to verbalize, not provide the verbalization for them.
Social interdependence theory requires genuine human peer collaboration; AI cannot replicate group accountability and shared social stakes.
Mechanism conflict risk: immediate answer reveal after wrong response bypasses necessary retrieval effort. AI must require retrieval before providing correct answer.
Social norm transmission, peer modeling, and belonging are embedded in physical peer community; AI cannot substitute.
Social-emotional teaching exchange, the act of one student teaching another, is the mechanism; AI monitoring of pairs is peripheral.
Social interdependence and group accountability require physical human presence; AI cannot replicate genuine cooperative stakes.
Embodied family relationships, warmth, stability, and shared routines are the mechanism; AI has no meaningful role in the causal chain.
Empathetic, trusting interpersonal connection is the causal mechanism. AI automating grading can free teacher time for relationships; AI cannot be the relationship.
Mechanism conflict: perpetual AI scaffolding of self-regulation prevents student from internalizing the regulatory capacity.
Embodied physical play, social negotiation, and intrinsic exploration cannot be replicated by AI-mediated interaction.
AI portals can streamline school-home communication; parental academic encouragement and home learning support are irreducibly human.
Critical mechanism conflict: AI note-taking (transcription) removes the encoding process that generates the learning benefit. Student must produce the notes.
Mechanism conflict: the act of discriminating important from unimportant information is the learning process; AI doing it removes the benefit.
Mechanism conflict risk: AI must require student to articulate goals, not supply goals to the student.
Socratic live questioning that adapts to student verbal responses in real-time requires human teacher; AI generates practice question sets.

Legitimate AI-native zone only with curriculum constraint, learner modeling, step-level feedback, and teacher oversight. Real-world compliance collapses without human enforcement. Vendor claims of Bloom's two-sigma solution are unsupported.
Relational safety and peer accountability within small groups require physical co-presence; AI facilitation degrades the mechanism.
Mechanism conflict: AI must scaffold practice of study skills, not execute study skills for the student.
High mechanism conflict risk: AI ghostwriting is the antithesis of the intervention. AI must evaluate and respond to student-produced writing, not produce writing for students.
Student-generated mental imagery is the mechanism; AI can prompt and guide the process but must not substitute images for the student.
Social belonging, shared identity, and collective classroom culture require physical co-presence and human teacher cultivation.
AI generates diverse examples for pattern discovery; teacher facilitation of the inductive reasoning discussion is the human layer.
Outdoor challenge, physical risk management, and group resilience-building are entirely embodied; AI has no meaningful role.
AI blind-grading and pattern detection can surface implicit low expectations; teacher must actively recalibrate their beliefs and behaviors.
Community of inquiry requires human facilitation of genuine philosophical dialogue; AI can supply texts and seed questions.
Mechanism conflict: AI must require student to generate explanations, not provide explanations to the student.
Critical mechanism conflict: AI writing tools that produce text for students are the antithesis of this intervention. Legitimate only as feedback and editing tools on student-produced writing.
Legitimate AI-native zone for procedural and content delivery; mechanism conflict risk high in writing and problem-solving tasks.
Mechanism conflict risk: AI must support student commitment to self-set goals, not set goals for students.
Positive interdependence, individual accountability within groups, and face-to-face interaction are the mechanisms; AI cannot replicate.
AI can surface resources and scaffold inquiry structure; genuine inquiry reasoning and question-formation require human facilitation.
